title: Aussies can bank on knowing what the RBA will do next
full_text:
We keep hearing about more RBA rate hikes to come.
Some commentators say it’s not a matter of if it hikes, but how many more times. But somewhere, below all the noise, is the droning sound of the banks quietly going about their business.
Banks are a bit like bees in a hive, and the Queen Bee is of course the RBA. Once the Queen makes a call, the bees take action. If one lender cuts rates, a bunch of others follow.
When there’s an RBA hike, one bank will dip its toe in the honey by passing it on to customers, and then the others swarm on board with hikes of their own.
But unlike real bees, the banks are in it for themselves.
If you want to gauge what’s going to happen, shut out the noise and pay attention to what banks actually do.
In the past week we saw ANZ and Macquarie both slash interest rates on their fixed term products. Days later, Canstar revealed 11 lenders had cut their variable rates for first homebuyer loans.
These don’t sound like the actions of banks who think rates will rise again. Canstar data director Sally Tindall said it’s important to be aware of what is happening in the lending market.
“Watching what banks do can provide useful clues about where interest rates might head next,” Ms Tindall said.
“Banks are constantly assessing their funding costs, broader economic conditions and competitive pressures in the mortgage market. While their economists are analysing the same inflation, employment and consumer spending data as the Reserve Bank, lenders also have a unique insight into what’s happening within their own loan books.
“We’re seeing some banks take the knife to variable and fixed rates in a bid to entice new customers.” In other words, pay attention to what banks are doing, because they know something we don’t.
Borrowers always have opportunities to ensure they are getting the best deal but a return of competition between lenders provides an even wider window.
“Those willing to shop around or negotiate with their lender may be able to unlock a better deal,” Ms Tindall said.
The bank moves come as pressure is growing on the RBA to cut the cash rate. Dale Gillham, chief analyst at Wealth Within pointed to rising unemployment and easing oil prices as a reason for a borrower boost.
“With households and businesses already feeling the impact of past rate hikes, the RBA risks turning a slowdown into a recession if it waits too long to cut,” Mr Gillham said.
“This month’s interest rate decision is shaping up as one of the easiest calls the RBA has had in years, yet policymakers sit frozen as the economy weakens around them.
“The warning signs are already here, and the cracks in the economy are becoming impossible to ignore. “At some point, the focus must shift from fighting inflation to protecting growth, because if unemployment keeps rising while productivity keeps falling, the economy won’t need another rate hike, it will need a rescue package.”
The Telegraph-20260614-Telegraph-86-000501807405

heading: Banking and Economy
